paragraphs.text:
## Pb²⁺ binders in SRD-46: what looks strongest, and what is clinical?

Using **SRD-46** for **Pb²⁺ (`metal_125`)**, the strongest *database-supported* binders under a matched condition set around **25 °C, I ≈ 0.1 M** are dominated by **large aza-macrocycles** and a few strong multidentate chelators. However, the ligands that appear strongest in the database are **mostly research ligands, not routine clinical antidotes**.

Using **SRD-46**

However, the ligands that appear strongest in the database are **mostly research ligands,
not routine clinical antidotes**.

paragraphs.text:
### Best-supported high-affinity Pb²⁺ ligands in SRD-46
| Rank tier | Ligand ID | Ligand | Ligand class | Pb²⁺ evidence in SRD-46 | Clinical use for lead poisoning? |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Highest-affinity shortlist | `ligand_7541` | [36]aneN12 | large polyaza macrocycle | Strong Pb²⁺ binder; **12 Pb²⁺ measurements** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | No evidence of standard clinical use |
| Highest-affinity shortlist | `ligand_7540` | [33]aneN11 | large polyaza macrocycle | Strong Pb²⁺ binder; **11 Pb²⁺ measurements** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | No evidence of standard clinical use |
| Strong comparison ligand | `ligand_6102` | DOTA | macrocyclic polyaminocarboxylate | Pb²⁺ stability data present; **4 measurements** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | Clinically used in radiopharmaceutical chemistry, **not standard for lead poisoning** |
| Strong comparison ligand | `ligand_6040` | N,N'-Bis(2-mercaptoethyl)ethylenedinitrilodiacetic acid | sulfur-donor chelator | Pb²⁺ stability data present; **2 measurements** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | Not a standard clinical chelator |
| Benchmark aminopolycarboxylate | `ligand_6301` | CDTA | EDTA-class aminopolycarboxylate | Pb²⁺ data present; **1 measurement** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | Benchmark only, not standard clinical use |
| Benchmark aminopolycarboxylate | `ligand_6277` | EDTA | aminopolycarboxylate | Pb²⁺ data present; **1 measurement** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | **Yes clinically as CaNa₂EDTA**, though the SRD entry is equilibrium data, not a treatment recommendation |
| Benchmark aminopolycarboxylate | `ligand_6356` | DTPA | aminopolycarboxylate | Pb²⁺ data present; **1 measurement** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | Not standard first-line for lead poisoning |
| Moderate benchmark | `ligand_6165` | NTA | aminopolycarboxylate | Pb²⁺ data present; **1 measurement** at 25 °C, 0.1 M | No |
| Baseline sulfur ligand | `ligand_5856` | Cysteine | thiol amino acid | Pb²⁺ data present; **1 matched-condition measurement** | No |

paragraphs.text:
### What this means practically
| Takeaway | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Strongest in-database binders | The **polyaza macrocycles** `[36]aneN12` and `[33]aneN11` are the most prominent high-affinity Pb²⁺ binders in SRD-46 under matched conditions |
| Best-known clinical ligand represented by Pb²⁺ equilibrium data | **EDTA** is the clinically relevant standout, specifically **CaNa₂EDTA** in practice |
| Thiol chemistry matters | Sulfur-donor ligands appear because **Pb²⁺ is thiophilic**, so thiol-containing ligands are chemically plausible strong binders even when not top-ranked by the limited matched-condition subset |
| Gap in this database slice | **Succimer (DMSA), dimercaprol (BAL), and DMPS** were **not recovered cleanly in the ligand-name resolution/searches here**, so SRD-46 does not support ranking them from the retrieved records in this session |

| Best-known clinical ligand represented by Pb²⁺ equilibrium data | **EDTA** is the clinically relevant standout, specifically **CaNa₂EDTA** in practice |

| Thiol chemistry matters | Sulfur-donor ligands appear because **Pb²⁺ is thiophilic**, so thiol-containing ligands are chemically plausible strong binders even when not top-ranked by the limited matched-condition subset |

| Gap in this database slice | **Succimer (DMSA), dimercaprol (BAL), and DMPS** were **not recovered cleanly in the ligand-name resolution/searches here**, so SRD-46 does not support ranking them from the retrieved records in this session |

paragraphs.text:
### Clinical-use summary
| Ligand | Clinical status for lead poisoning | Supported here? |
|---|---|---|
| EDTA / CaNa₂EDTA | **Yes**, established clinical chelator for lead poisoning | Supported as an SRD-46 Pb²⁺ ligand; clinical-use statement is conventional medical knowledge |
| Succimer / DMSA | **Yes**, established clinical chelator | **Not retrievable from the SRD-46 results obtained here** |
| Dimercaprol / BAL | **Yes**, used clinically, especially in some severe cases with EDTA | **Not retrievable from the SRD-46 results obtained here** |
| DMPS | Clinical use exists in some settings/regions, but less standard than EDTA or succimer | **Not supported by retrieved SRD-46 records here** |
| Penicillamine | Historically used for some metals, **not standard first-line for lead** | Only indirect hinting in the catalog, not a top Pb²⁺ affinity result here |

paragraphs.text:
## Bottom line
If you ask strictly **which ligands in SRD-46 show the highest Pb²⁺ affinity**, the top hits from the retrieved data are **large aza-macrocycles** such as **`ligand_7541` ([36]aneN12)** and **`ligand_7540` ([33]aneN11)**. These are **not standard clinical lead-chelation drugs**.

If you ask **which clinically used ligands are relevant**, the clearest answer is **EDTA (clinically as CaNa₂EDTA)**. **Succimer (DMSA)** and **dimercaprol (BAL)** are clinically important for lead poisoning, but that clinical relevance was **not directly recoverable from the SRD-46 records retrieved in this run**, so I would not claim an SRD-46-based affinity ranking for them.

SRD-46 source support retained in this session included Pb–ligand stability literature such as **62SBb, 69T, 69BS, 70BWa/70BWb, 69DMB, 67MRM, 72CK**.
